title: Ethan at 2 Months
subtitle: A checkpoint-style guide for early smiles, sounds, and connection.
overview: Around 2 months, many babies become more alert, more responsive to faces and voices, and more expressive in small ways. Every baby develops on their own timeline.

developmentalTopics.title: Faces and smiles
developmentalTopics.body: You may start to notice more alert face-watching, calmer moments with familiar voices, and early smiles that feel social.

developmentalTopics.title: Tiny conversations
developmentalTopics.body: Listen for coos, soft sounds, and little pauses when you talk. Capture the moments that feel new or especially Ethan.

developmentalTopics.title: Head, hands, and tummy time
developmentalTopics.body: Many babies around this age are still building head control and body strength. Short, supervised tummy time can create memory-worthy effort.

milestonePrompts.title: First social smile
milestonePrompts.promptText: Did Ethan give a smile that felt especially connected?

milestonePrompts.title: First coo conversation
milestonePrompts.promptText: Did Ethan start a little back-and-forth with coos or sounds?

milestonePrompts.title: New favorite book
milestonePrompts.promptText: Was there a book, song, or voice that got a new reaction?

playIdeas: Talk face-to-face and pause as if you are having a tiny conversation.
Try a short book, song, or high-contrast toy during a calm awake window.
Use short, supervised tummy time when Ethan is alert and comfortable.
careNotes: Keep notes gentle: these are memory prompts, not a scorecard.
Bring questions or patterns that concern you to Ethan's pediatrician.
sleepNotes: Sleep can change quickly around growth, illness, and routine shifts.
Look for patterns over several days rather than one difficult nap or night.
feedingNotes: Feeding rhythms can shift as Ethan grows.
Ask the pediatrician if you have concerns about intake, comfort, or growth.
safetyNotes: Keep floor and sleep spaces simple and safe.
Re-check surfaces where Ethan lies or is changed as movement increases.

sourceReferences.sourceName: CDC Learn the Signs. Act Early.
sourceReferences.notes: Used as the preferred basis for checkpoint-age milestone themes.

sourceReferences.sourceName: HealthyChildren.org by the American Academy of Pediatrics
sourceReferences.notes: Used for parent-facing development, care, play, feeding, and safety framing.

disclaimer: Monthly guides are based on general developmental information and your logged data. They are not medical advice. Ask your pediatrician if you have concerns about Ethan's development.

title: Ethan at 3 Months
subtitle: A between-checkpoint guide for play, hands, sounds, and routines.
overview: Around 3 months, you may notice more social interaction, stronger head control, and new interest in hands, sounds, and nearby toys.

developmentalTopics.title: More interactive moments
developmentalTopics.body: Watch for new expressions, attention to familiar people, and moments where Ethan seems to anticipate a routine or game.

developmentalTopics.title: Sounds with personality
developmentalTopics.body: This can be a lovely month to capture favorite coos, squeals, pauses, or reactions to songs.

developmentalTopics.title: Hands and simple play
developmentalTopics.body: Many babies become more interested in their hands and nearby toys. Notice what Ethan looks toward, bats at, or seems to enjoy.

milestonePrompts.title: Held hands together at center
milestonePrompts.promptText: Did Ethan bring hands together, study them, or hold them at center?

milestonePrompts.title: New favorite sound
milestonePrompts.promptText: What sound, squeal, coo, or little noise stood out this month?

milestonePrompts.title: New funny habit
milestonePrompts.promptText: What tiny habit made you laugh this month?

playIdeas: Offer a safe toy slightly to the side and watch for looking, reaching, or batting.
Narrate diaper changes or outfit changes and wait for Ethan's response.
Try a short book or song and capture any new reaction as a memory.
careNotes: Use the guide to notice patterns and memories, not to grade development.
Save questions for the next pediatrician visit if something worries you.
sleepNotes: Sleep rhythms can be uneven while babies grow and practice new skills.
Use your logs to spot patterns over time.
feedingNotes: Feeding patterns may vary with growth, distraction, or routine changes.
Ask the pediatrician if feeding or growth concerns come up.
safetyNotes: As Ethan gets more active, keep changing surfaces guarded and never leave him unattended on raised surfaces.
Check toys and play spaces for small parts or loose pieces.

sourceReferences.sourceName: HealthyChildren.org by the American Academy of Pediatrics
sourceReferences.notes: Used for parent-facing development, care, play, feeding, and safety framing.

disclaimer: Monthly guides are based on general developmental information and your logged data. They are not medical advice. Ask your pediatrician if you have concerns about Ethan's development.

title: Ethan at 4 Months
subtitle: What to expect around 4 months, with playful memory prompts.
overview: Around this age, many babies become more expressive, more interested in faces and voices, and more active with their hands and body. Every baby develops on their own timeline.

developmentalTopics.title: More expressive connection
developmentalTopics.body: You may start to notice bigger reactions to familiar people, playful expressions, or moments that feel like real back-and-forth.

developmentalTopics.title: Laughs, squeals, and favorite sounds
developmentalTopics.body: Many babies around this age experiment with sounds. Capture the first sounds that feel especially memorable.

developmentalTopics.title: Hands, reaching, and body practice
developmentalTopics.body: You may notice reaching, hands to mouth, body wiggles, rolling attempts, or stronger head and chest lifting during supervised play.

developmentalTopics.title: Routines can shift
developmentalTopics.body: Sleep and feeding patterns can change around new skills, growth, and daily rhythm shifts. The logs are useful for patterns, not single-day verdicts.

milestonePrompts.title: First big laugh
milestonePrompts.promptText: Did Ethan laugh, squeal, or light up in a new way?

milestonePrompts.title: Held hands together at center
milestonePrompts.promptText: Did Ethan bring hands together or to the mouth?

milestonePrompts.title: Reached for a toy
milestonePrompts.promptText: Did Ethan reach, swipe, grab, or show a new favorite toy?

milestonePrompts.title: Sized up diapers or clothes
milestonePrompts.promptText: Did Ethan move into a new diaper or clothing size?

playIdeas: Read one short book and watch for smiles, sounds, or focused looking.
Offer a safe toy nearby and notice reaching, batting, or grabbing attempts.
Try a few minutes of supervised tummy time with a favorite face or toy in view.
Sing the same little song for a few days and capture any new reaction.
careNotes: Capture memories in your own words; the guide is here to prompt, not pressure.
Bring questions or concerns to Ethan's pediatrician.
sleepNotes: Some babies have shifting sleep around this stage. Look for multi-day patterns in logs.
If sleep changes feel sudden or concerning, use notes for the pediatrician.
feedingNotes: Feeding can be affected by distraction, growth, or routine changes.
Ask the pediatrician before making major feeding changes.
safetyNotes: Rolling attempts can surprise you; re-check changing tables, couches, beds, and play spaces.
Keep small objects and loose pieces away from play areas.

sourceReferences.sourceName: CDC Learn the Signs. Act Early.
sourceReferences.notes: Used as the preferred basis for checkpoint-age milestone themes.

sourceReferences.sourceName: HealthyChildren.org by the American Academy of Pediatrics
sourceReferences.notes: Used for parent-facing development, care, play, feeding, and safety framing.

disclaimer: Monthly guides are based on general developmental information and your logged data. They are not medical advice. Ask your pediatrician if you have concerns about Ethan's development.

title: Ethan at 5 Months
subtitle: A between-checkpoint guide for curiosity, reaching, and routines.
overview: Around 5 months, this can be a playful in-between stage: more reaching, more sound-making, and more curiosity about routines and people.

developmentalTopics.title: Curiosity and repetition
developmentalTopics.body: Many babies enjoy simple repeated games, familiar songs, and toys they can look at or reach toward.

developmentalTopics.title: Sounds with rhythm
developmentalTopics.body: You may notice favorite sounds, louder reactions, or little sound routines that happen during play or care.

developmentalTopics.title: Practice, attempts, and almosts
developmentalTopics.body: This is a good month to capture attempts: almost rolling, reaching with more purpose, or new body positions.

milestonePrompts.title: New favorite toy
milestonePrompts.promptText: Did Ethan clearly prefer a toy, book, song, or game?

milestonePrompts.title: Rolled or tried to roll
milestonePrompts.promptText: Did Ethan roll, twist, or work hard toward rolling?

milestonePrompts.title: New funny habit
milestonePrompts.promptText: What new face, sound, or routine made everyone laugh?

playIdeas: Place a safe toy within reach and wait before helping.
Repeat a favorite song and watch for anticipation.
Use mirror play or face-to-face play during a calm awake window.
careNotes: Between-checkpoint months are perfect for small memories and questions.
Ask Ethan's pediatrician if you have concerns.
sleepNotes: New movement practice can show up around sleep.
Look for patterns across several days.
feedingNotes: Feeding interest and distraction can shift at this age.
Use notes to track questions for the pediatrician.
safetyNotes: Assume movement can change quickly and keep raised surfaces guarded.
Check play spaces for small objects before floor time.

sourceReferences.sourceName: HealthyChildren.org by the American Academy of Pediatrics
sourceReferences.notes: Used for parent-facing development, care, play, feeding, and safety framing.

disclaimer: Monthly guides are based on general developmental information and your logged data. They are not medical advice. Ask your pediatrician if you have concerns about Ethan's development.

title: Ethan at 6 Months
subtitle: A checkpoint-style guide for interaction, movement, feeding notes, and memories.
overview: Around 6 months, many babies are increasingly interactive and physical. This is often a helpful month to capture new sounds, movement, feeding changes, and favorite games.

developmentalTopics.title: Big reactions and familiar people
developmentalTopics.body: Notice the faces, voices, songs, and people that reliably get a reaction from Ethan.

developmentalTopics.title: Babbling and sound play
developmentalTopics.body: Many babies around this age experiment with repeated sounds and expressive noises. Capture the sounds you never want to forget.

developmentalTopics.title: Feeding memories and questions
developmentalTopics.body: If solids are part of Ethan's care plan, this can be a good place to capture first tastes and reactions. Ask the pediatrician about feeding questions.

developmentalTopics.title: Rolling, sitting, and reaching
developmentalTopics.body: You may notice new attempts with rolling, sitting with support, reaching, or transferring toys. Capture attempts and surprises.

milestonePrompts.title: Tried a new food
milestonePrompts.promptText: Did Ethan explore a new taste, texture, or feeding routine?

milestonePrompts.title: Sat with support
milestonePrompts.promptText: Was there a new sitting, rolling, or reaching moment?

milestonePrompts.title: New babble or sound
milestonePrompts.promptText: What new sound or repeated noise showed up this month?

playIdeas: Put a safe toy just out of reach and let Ethan work toward it.
Read a book with faces, rhythm, or repeated sounds.
Capture first food reactions if solids are part of Ethan's care plan.
Try supported sitting play on the floor with close supervision.
careNotes: This guide is for noticing and remembering, not diagnosing.
Bring development, feeding, or sleep questions to Ethan's pediatrician.
sleepNotes: New mobility and routines can affect sleep for a while.
Compare patterns across days instead of reacting to one rough stretch.
feedingNotes: If solids are part of Ethan's care plan, log first tastes as memories.
Ask the pediatrician before major feeding changes or if you have concerns.
safetyNotes: Re-check floor spaces, small objects, cords, and furniture as movement increases.
Use safe, supervised positioning for sitting and play.

sourceReferences.sourceName: CDC Learn the Signs. Act Early.
sourceReferences.notes: Used as the preferred basis for checkpoint-age milestone themes.

sourceReferences.sourceName: HealthyChildren.org by the American Academy of Pediatrics
sourceReferences.notes: Used for parent-facing development, care, play, feeding, and safety framing.

disclaimer: Monthly guides are based on general developmental information and your logged data. They are not medical advice. Ask your pediatrician if you have concerns about Ethan's development.

title: Ethan at 7 Months
subtitle: A between-checkpoint guide for sitting practice, curiosity, and first food memories.
overview: Around 7 months, many babies are busy practicing body control, exploring objects, and getting more opinionated about favorite people, games, and routines.

developmentalTopics.title: Sitting, rolling, and reaching practice
developmentalTopics.body: This can be a month of almosts: longer sitting moments, rolling with more intention, reaching across the body, or working toward a toy.

developmentalTopics.title: Exploring objects
developmentalTopics.body: Notice what Ethan studies, mouths, shakes, drops, bangs, or reaches for again and again.

developmentalTopics.title: Food reactions and family routines
developmentalTopics.body: If solids are part of Ethan's care plan, this is a sweet time to capture favorite tastes, dramatic faces, and mealtime firsts.

milestonePrompts.title: Longest sitting moment so far
milestonePrompts.promptText: Did Ethan sit, balance, wobble, or recover in a new way?

milestonePrompts.title: A memorable food face
milestonePrompts.promptText: Was there a taste, texture, or reaction you want to remember?

milestonePrompts.title: New favorite object
milestonePrompts.promptText: What toy, spoon, book, cup, or everyday object kept getting Ethan's attention?

playIdeas: Place a safe toy just out of reach and give Ethan time to problem-solve.
Try floor play with sitting support nearby and close supervision.
Name objects Ethan is touching, shaking, or mouthing.
Capture funny food reactions if solids are part of Ethan's routine.
careNotes: Use this month for small memories, not comparisons.
Bring feeding, movement, or routine questions to Ethan's pediatrician.
sleepNotes: Movement practice and feeding changes can coincide with sleep shifts.
Look at patterns across several days before changing routines.
feedingNotes: Follow Ethan's pediatrician's guidance for solids, allergens, and textures.
Log new foods as memories and as useful context for care conversations.
safetyNotes: Re-check floor spaces for small objects, cords, furniture edges, and reachable items.
Keep sitting and feeding practice supervised and age-appropriate.

sourceReferences.sourceName: HealthyChildren.org by the American Academy of Pediatrics
sourceReferences.notes: Used for parent-facing development, care, play, feeding, and safety framing.

disclaimer: Monthly guides are based on general developmental information and your logged data. They are not medical advice. Ask your pediatrician if you have concerns about Ethan's development.

title: Ethan at 8 Months
subtitle: A between-checkpoint guide for movement attempts, object play, and familiar routines.
overview: Around 8 months, babies may be more mobile in their own way, more curious about cause and effect, and more expressive about familiar people and routines.

developmentalTopics.title: Familiar people and big reactions
developmentalTopics.body: Notice how Ethan responds to favorite people, peekaboo, songs, greetings, and daily routines.

developmentalTopics.title: Cause and effect
developmentalTopics.body: Dropping, banging, shaking, and repeating actions can be part of learning how the world responds.

developmentalTopics.title: Scooting, pivoting, reaching, or trying
developmentalTopics.body: Capture Ethan's movement style without needing it to fit a label: pivoting, rolling, pushing up, scooting, rocking, or reaching all count as memories.

milestonePrompts.title: New way of moving
milestonePrompts.promptText: Did Ethan find a new way to get closer to something interesting?

milestonePrompts.title: Favorite repeated game
milestonePrompts.promptText: What game, sound, or routine did Ethan want again and again?

milestonePrompts.title: Special person reaction
milestonePrompts.promptText: Was there a greeting, laugh, reach, or expression for someone special?

playIdeas: Play peekaboo or a repeated song and pause for Ethan's reaction.
Offer two safe objects and notice which one Ethan chooses.
Use sturdy floor play spaces for supervised reaching and movement practice.
Narrate cause and effect: shake, drop, bang, open, close.
careNotes: This guide is a memory prompt, not a developmental grade.
Save questions for Ethan's pediatrician if something feels concerning.
sleepNotes: Separation, new skills, illness, or schedule changes can affect sleep.
Use the logs to notice whether a change is temporary or becoming a pattern.
feedingNotes: Feeding interest can vary with distraction, teething, growth, and routine.
Ask the pediatrician about food texture, allergen, or intake questions.
safetyNotes: Assume reach and movement can change overnight; re-check babyproofing often.
Keep cords, small objects, hot drinks, and unstable furniture out of reach.

sourceReferences.sourceName: HealthyChildren.org by the American Academy of Pediatrics
sourceReferences.notes: Used for parent-facing development, care, play, feeding, and safety framing.

disclaimer: Monthly guides are based on general developmental information and your logged data. They are not medical advice. Ask your pediatrician if you have concerns about Ethan's development.

title: Ethan at 9 Months
subtitle: A checkpoint-style guide for gestures, object play, movement, and memory-worthy routines.
overview: Around 9 months, many babies show more back-and-forth interaction, more purposeful object play, and more determined movement practice. Every baby develops on their own timeline.

developmentalTopics.title: Connection, preferences, and reactions
developmentalTopics.body: You may notice stronger reactions to familiar people, favorite games, or moments when Ethan looks for a response from you.

developmentalTopics.title: Sounds, gestures, and attention
developmentalTopics.body: Capture new babbles, gestures, reaching to be picked up, or moments when Ethan responds to a name, song, or familiar phrase.

developmentalTopics.title: Looking, finding, and object play
developmentalTopics.body: Many babies around this age are interested in finding hidden objects, passing toys between hands, and exploring containers or cause and effect.

developmentalTopics.title: Sitting and moving with purpose
developmentalTopics.body: Notice Ethan's own movement style: sitting, pivoting, scooting, crawling attempts, pulling toward things, or reaching with more control.

milestonePrompts.title: New gesture or signal
milestonePrompts.promptText: Did Ethan wave, reach, lift arms, point, clap, or use a new signal?

milestonePrompts.title: Found a hidden object
milestonePrompts.promptText: Did Ethan look for something after it disappeared or play a new hiding game?

milestonePrompts.title: Moved with purpose
milestonePrompts.promptText: What movement got Ethan closer to a person, toy, or place?

milestonePrompts.title: Favorite babble
milestonePrompts.promptText: What repeated sound or babble felt especially Ethan this month?

playIdeas: Hide a toy partly under a cloth and watch what Ethan tries.
Play turn-taking games with clapping, waving, peekaboo, or repeating sounds.
Offer safe containers for putting objects in and taking them out.
Create supervised floor space for movement practice.
careNotes: A checkpoint guide is a prompt for noticing and questions, not a diagnosis.
Bring any development, hearing, vision, feeding, or movement concerns to Ethan's pediatrician.
sleepNotes: New mobility, separation feelings, illness, or travel can affect sleep.
Compare sleep over several days and keep notes for care conversations.
feedingNotes: If solids are part of Ethan's care plan, mealtime may become more interactive.
Ask the pediatrician about textures, allergens, choking safety, or intake concerns.
safetyNotes: Re-check babyproofing for movement, pulling, reaching, and exploring.
Keep small objects, cords, hot drinks, medicines, and cleaning products out of reach.

sourceReferences.sourceName: CDC Learn the Signs. Act Early.
sourceReferences.notes: Used as the preferred basis for checkpoint-age milestone themes.

sourceReferences.sourceName: HealthyChildren.org by the American Academy of Pediatrics
sourceReferences.notes: Used for parent-facing development, care, play, feeding, and safety framing.

disclaimer: Monthly guides are based on general developmental information and your logged data. They are not medical advice. Ask your pediatrician if you have concerns about Ethan's development.

title: Ethan at 10 Months
subtitle: A between-checkpoint guide for exploring, sounds, routines, and little preferences.
overview: Around 10 months, many babies are deep into exploration: moving toward favorite things, testing cause and effect, practicing sounds, and showing very specific preferences.

developmentalTopics.title: Little experiments
developmentalTopics.body: Dropping, opening, closing, banging, handing things over, or repeating a trick can all become memorable experiments.

developmentalTopics.title: Sounds that mean something
developmentalTopics.body: Listen for favorite syllables, emphatic noises, gestures, or sounds that seem tied to a person, object, or routine.

developmentalTopics.title: Moving through the day
developmentalTopics.body: Whether Ethan is rolling, scooting, crawling, pulling, cruising attempts, or doing something entirely his own, capture the style and the story.

milestonePrompts.title: Favorite experiment
milestonePrompts.promptText: What did Ethan repeat until everyone noticed?

milestonePrompts.title: Word-like sound
milestonePrompts.promptText: Was there a sound that seemed attached to a person, object, or moment?

milestonePrompts.title: Pulling or standing attempt
milestonePrompts.promptText: Did Ethan try to pull up, stand with help, cruise, or reach higher?

playIdeas: Use simple containers for in-and-out play.
Name people and objects during repeated routines.
Offer safe furniture-free floor space for movement.
Read books with flaps, faces, rhythm, or repeated phrases.
careNotes: Capture what Ethan loves, not just what he achieves.
Bring questions or concerns to the pediatrician.
sleepNotes: Practice with movement, standing, or separation can show up at bedtime.
Use sleep logs to understand whether the pattern is changing.
feedingNotes: Mealtime may include stronger preferences and more participation.
Ask the pediatrician about textures, choking safety, allergens, or intake concerns.
safetyNotes: Re-check furniture, cords, outlets, stairs, cabinets, and table edges.
Keep small objects and unsafe foods out of reach.

sourceReferences.sourceName: HealthyChildren.org by the American Academy of Pediatrics
sourceReferences.notes: Used for parent-facing development, care, play, feeding, and safety framing.

disclaimer: Monthly guides are based on general developmental information and your logged data. They are not medical advice. Ask your pediatrician if you have concerns about Ethan's development.

title: Ethan at 11 Months
subtitle: A between-checkpoint guide for pre-toddler energy, gestures, and family memories.
overview: Around 11 months, many babies feel wonderfully specific: favorite games, favorite people, stronger opinions, new movement attempts, and expressive sounds.

developmentalTopics.title: Preferences and personality
developmentalTopics.body: Notice the games, people, songs, foods, and routines Ethan clearly likes, dislikes, anticipates, or requests.

developmentalTopics.title: Gestures and shared attention
developmentalTopics.body: Capture pointing, reaching, waving, clapping, showing objects, or looking back to share a moment.

developmentalTopics.title: Almost a toddler, still Ethan's pace
developmentalTopics.body: Movement can look very different baby to baby. Log the memorable attempts, not comparisons.

milestonePrompts.title: Clear favorite
milestonePrompts.promptText: What person, food, song, toy, or routine was unmistakably a favorite?

milestonePrompts.title: Shared a moment
milestonePrompts.promptText: Did Ethan look back, show something, clap, wave, or invite you into a game?

milestonePrompts.title: New routine personality
milestonePrompts.promptText: What care routine now has a very Ethan-specific reaction?

playIdeas: Practice simple turn-taking with a ball, block, song, or repeated sound.
Name body parts, people, foods, and familiar objects during routines.
Offer sturdy supervised play spaces for pulling, standing, cruising, or movement.
Capture the funniest tiny opinion of the month.
careNotes: This is a good month to write down favorites before they change.
Ask the pediatrician about any development, feeding, sleep, or safety concerns.
sleepNotes: Separation, mobility, and routine preferences can affect sleep.
Track patterns and note what helps.
feedingNotes: Food preferences can be strong and changeable.
Ask the pediatrician about textures, nutrition, choking safety, or intake questions.
safetyNotes: Assume Ethan can reach more than yesterday.
Re-check furniture stability, stairs, cords, outlets, drawers, and table surfaces.

sourceReferences.sourceName: HealthyChildren.org by the American Academy of Pediatrics
sourceReferences.notes: Used for parent-facing development, care, play, feeding, and safety framing.

disclaimer: Monthly guides are based on general developmental information and your logged data. They are not medical advice. Ask your pediatrician if you have concerns about Ethan's development.

title: Ethan at 12 Months
subtitle: A checkpoint-style first-birthday guide for gestures, words, movement, play, and memories.
overview: Around 12 months, many babies are becoming more intentional communicators and explorers. The first birthday is also a wonderful moment to capture what Ethan loves right now.

developmentalTopics.title: Games, help, and shared moments
developmentalTopics.body: Notice how Ethan participates in favorite games, offers objects, seeks comfort, or reacts to familiar people and routines.

developmentalTopics.title: Gestures and word-like sounds
developmentalTopics.body: Capture waves, pointing, reaching, meaningful sounds, favorite syllables, and any words or almost-words you want to remember.

developmentalTopics.title: Using objects with intention
developmentalTopics.body: Many babies around this age imitate actions, search for things, put objects in containers, and use familiar items in purposeful ways.

developmentalTopics.title: Standing, cruising, crawling, or walking attempts
developmentalTopics.body: Movement varies a lot. Capture Ethan's style: crawling, cruising, standing, assisted steps, first steps, or another determined approach.

milestonePrompts.title: First birthday memory
milestonePrompts.promptText: What detail from Ethan's first birthday do you want to keep forever?

milestonePrompts.title: First word or almost-word
milestonePrompts.promptText: Was there a word, name, sound, or phrase that seemed meaningful?

milestonePrompts.title: Standing, cruising, or steps
milestonePrompts.promptText: Did Ethan stand, cruise, crawl somewhere important, take assisted steps, or take first steps?

milestonePrompts.title: One-year favorites
milestonePrompts.promptText: What are Ethan's favorite foods, songs, books, toys, people, and games at one year?

playIdeas: Create a one-year favorites memory: song, book, food, toy, face, and game.
Practice simple imitation with clapping, waving, stacking, putting in, and taking out.
Read books with familiar objects and pause for pointing, sounds, or gestures.
Offer safe supervised spaces for crawling, cruising, standing, or walking practice.
careNotes: Use the first birthday as a memory checkpoint and a place to collect pediatrician questions.
This guide is not medical advice or a developmental diagnosis.
sleepNotes: Travel, celebrations, routines, and mobility can all affect sleep.
Use logs to spot patterns and bring concerns to the pediatrician.
feedingNotes: Food routines may shift around the first birthday; follow Ethan's pediatrician's guidance.
Keep logging favorites, reactions, and questions about nutrition, textures, or intake.
safetyNotes: Re-check babyproofing for climbing, pulling, cruising, and opening cabinets.
Review choking hazards, safe foods, medicines, cleaning products, and water safety.

sourceReferences.notes: Used as the preferred basis for checkpoint-age milestone themes.
